title: What an American thinks about Latvia

transcript.text: Now this video is what an American thinks about Latvia. I was only in Latvia one day. Got there late at night one day, spent the night there, and then spent the whole next day there and then took the night bus into Estonia, in Riga.

So, yeah, well, taking the bus across Latvia, I saw the countryside. It was flat, totally flat. Lots of forests. You've got lots of pine forests and flatness. And off in the distance, you can see those communist, big old communist apartment blocks that can house a million people and not so much space.
Um, Riga was beautiful. I was really impressed with Riga. Riga's downtown area, well, it's a UNESCO World Heritage Site, but Riga's downtown area is like a freaking blast to the past.

It's like, uh, Prague, where it's all pedestrian, only Prague is like Disneyland. Riga, because they have all these huge stores there and cars everywhere, overcrowded, but Riga is like, there's no cars in the downtown.
I don't know if they can drive or if they won't. I think they could, but they just didn't. Cars just completely avoided downtown area of Riga, like for blocks and blocks.
And it's all really narrow, cobblestone streets. Um, you know, crisscrossing and going under buildings and, you know, under arches and stuff and lots of different, um, at least two, uh, church areas.

Like one with two churches next to them with like a kind of, with where they all, these little roads kind of convene and you got people selling stuff and, um, another church next couple blocks away with this big kind of, um, square area in front of it and a couple other big squares.

And then there's this river because they have a really big river going through Riga, but I, and most of it's on the northern side of Riga. I didn't go on the southern side of it. It looked like the city pretty much ended after the southern side, but on the river, on the north side of the river, where the city is, they have this only little river that they diverted that goes over like a canal.

And most of it is, it's, it's surrounded by, on either side by a big park with lots and lots and lots of people hanging out, walking down, sunbathing, you know, taking canoe rides down or even boat tour rides down it.
And lots of like bridges going over it. And, um, that was right next to the downtown area, the head to the historical downtown area, Cobbled, which is really, uh, really pretty and impressive.

I was there all day checking it out. And there's this, uh, big, like 30 story, 26 story building that I think is a hotel that's right next to downtown area that you can go all the way up to the top floor on their skyline bar and, uh, look over the whole city and, uh, um, do the panorama.

Now Riga is bigger, a lot bigger than, than Vilnius. Vilnius is a little city. Riga is, is a big city, but it's not as big as like the big, big cities, but it is a pretty big city, but you can see the end of it off in the distance, not so far.
So I guess medium-sized big city. I don't know the exact population, but it looked like north of where I was, though, it wouldn't have been that interesting to check out.
Just normal city roads and roads. But the part that I was in was really interesting. Um, and lots of tourists, tourists everywhere.
The hostel I stayed at, the, uh, the Naughty Squirrel, it was an Australian hostel.

So that's a weird thing about Australians, like, traveling, noticing, you notice, you notice a couple of things, some interesting things traveling that I noticed is that you have that, like, you know, certain countries export certain things, you know?
Now, one thing that the Irish export is, is pubs. Everywhere I go, there's the Irish pub, because I guess Irish have the best pubs.
So yeah, there's one in Riga, there's one everywhere. Irish pub, but I'm sure it's owned by Irish people.
So, he's like, um, so, and even people told me, recently on YouTube, they're like, you better go to Ireland and hang out in the pubs, man, pub life, you know?
So I guess they're really friendly. They like to party and hang out with you and stuff in Ireland.
But anyways, the Australians like to export the hostels.
They set up this hostel network, and they're like, come to the Australian hostel, mate.
So you go there, and it's all Australians.
So that's what the Naughty Squirrel is. It's the Australian hostel.
But there are lots of hostels like that. We're going, it's all Australians, because you all go to the same hostel.
But it was a cool hostel, and they had this hangout area, and the people who worked there were really jacky and wanted to help you out,
And give you the map and tell you where you go, and we sell beer, and, you know, make it a party hostel and stuff.
But, um, but, yeah, in Riga, next to, next to where I was, um, they have hangers, four different hangers in a row.
There are like seven hangers, and four of them in a row, and the other two, like three over there.
But, um, for making the, um, the, uh, those big balloons, um, I can't remember what they're called.
Yeah, but those big balloons that they used to use to get around.
Um, and I went in, and now they're, they're like supermarkets.
They've converted them into markets.
And I went in, it was like a supermarket and lots of stores and stuff in there.
And I was filming, and some lady in one of the side stores is like,
Not the charm!
And I was like, whoa, okay, sorry, I didn't even point it to you.
But then, like, some security guard lady, like, makes me stop filming.
So I guess they didn't want to film in there, which is interesting.
I don't know why the deal with that was.
Maybe they're ashamed that they're using hangers, because they're sore.
I don't know what the deal is.
But, um, maybe I'll study it on that.
But on the outside, they had, uh, some selling too.
So, but, and then I went to the other side, to the Russian side, a little Moscow.
And in the middle, they had this church that was this big, like, Empire State Building looking like building.
And I, they had one of those in, um, in Moscow, in, uh, Poland.
And I, I think Poland, was it part of USSR?
But anyways, I think, yeah, the Russians, I think, built that big building in Poland.
It was this big, like, um, building that kind of looks like, that looks really bland and gray and, like, brown.
And it kind of looks like the Empire State Building.
Somebody said they thought it was really ugly, but I guess the Russians put that there.
Maybe, they had one that was smaller than that.
So maybe that's what the Russians do.
They go in and build one of those buildings with their stamp on it.
But, uh, it was there in little Moscow.
I thought, I think they're pretty.
But, uh, it was, it was, it's cool to have a big building like that.
But anyways, being there was really, it's a lot different from the other part of town.
Even though it was really close, that sector, it was, had really dilapidated buildings.
Like, wooden buildings where they're so dilapidated, you can see them sagging all over the place and not painted at all.
And all the, all the, uh, the brick buildings not painted and the paint chipping off.
And a poor part.
And the guy in my hostel said, uh, I should check it out during the day, but at night, it's dangerous.
I won't go there at night.
Um, but, um, because it was part of the USSR and I guess they brought a bunch of Russians in there.
And so, uh, maybe they're the poor people.
So, but I didn't see many, um, I didn't see much of that.
I wasn't there for very long.
But it was, um, interesting because before I went there, it looked like Riga was really well kept up.
All the buildings were painted really well.
Um, the trails and roads were in good shape.
Um, so yeah, it looks like Latvia's doing well.
Latvia, I think that certain, one certain year in like 2003 or something,
It was the fastest growing country in, in GDP and, uh, the EU.
And that, but now it has like the highest crime rate or something.
Riga has the highest crime rate, uh, of any city in the EU.
Because they, and they also had, they'd fallen the hardest in one certain year or so.
Anyways, um, well, and I think it's the second poorest country in the EU.
But going through the tourist part of Riga, you wouldn't notice that.
You would just think it's a really cool place.
Until you go to the little Russia, little Moscow.
But, um, the big river next to Riga was really big.
Um, really giant river.
I don't know which one it was, but it was big.
But it was right next to the ocean.
Riga is.
Uh, I didn't see the ocean though.
So it's not right, the downtown area is not right next to the ocean.
I guess you'd have to go down a few miles.
But the city itself, I think, lines the ocean.
Um, but I didn't see it.
I don't, I think I did see the ocean.
I saw where the ocean was.
I saw the beach from the top of that 26-story hotel.
It was kind of hazy.
I didn't see it outlined perfectly.
But, um, the, some, enough of the people could speak English there in Riga.
They didn't use euros.
They used their own money.
Every country in, in, uh, the Baltics uses their own money.
Which is interesting for me because that means that, like, in, like, the course of, like, a week and a half, I had used, like, seven different kinds of currencies.
I used the Romanian currency, the Moldovan currency, Ukrainian, Polish, Latvia, Lithuanian, Stolium.
Seven different currencies in the course of, like, ten days.
Like, what is this money again?
So, anyways.
Um, yeah.
That's what I think about Riga.
Um, they have a huge nightlight there.
When I first got there, it was, like, 1230.
And I'm going to walk around.
And they were having this party still.
And this was, like, on a Tuesday night.
I mean, it was a Sunday night.
But there were all these people dancing in the Central Square, playing music.
And you walk around the streets and there's, like, people everywhere at 1230 at night on a Sunday night.
So, and it was still a little bit, it was, it was, I think it was dark by then, but it was a little bit light until, like, 1130.
So, yeah.
They're into the nightlife over there in Riga.
Um, yeah.
So that's what America thinks about that thing.
Yeah.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you.
